text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nayar
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nayar
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH783215 NHS No. 9473165820  
12 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 2JT

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. He works as a warehouse picker between tall racking and narrow aisles where lifting demands and pace vary by shift. He reports episodic events that have continued for several years, with the current pattern occurring q2 - 3wk. He describes a brief prodrome of feeling "spacey" and tingling in both hands, sometimes noting that lights appear overly bright in the aisle. Witness accounts from colleagues suggest he then becomes unresponsive for under a minute with blank staring, occasional lip movements, and afterwards appears confused and slow to answer for 20–40 minutes. There has been no witnessed tongue biting or incontinence. A minority of events occur on late shifts when the pace is highest. He has had two episodes in the loading bay where he sat down suddenly and could not recall the next 15 minutes; in both instances, a co-worker guided him to the break room and he recovered.

Seizure frequency is reported as q2 - 3wk over the past six months, with some clustering during consecutive late shifts. He denies clear sleep deprivation but says that overtime can shorten his sleep to about five hours on back-to-back nights. He drinks no alcohol on workdays and minimal at weekends. No recent head injuries have been reported. He has not driven for over a year and understands DVLA obligations.

Medication history: He is currently taking lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily with good adherence (he uses a phone alarm and blister packs). He reports mild nausea in the mornings which settles with food. He previously tried levetiracetam and stopped due to irritability after three weeks. No known drug allergies. He is not on any interacting medicines or supplements to his knowledge.

Examination in clinic today was unremarkable. There were no focal neurological deficits. Cardiovascular and basic observations were normal. Routine bloods from his GP last month (FBC, U&E, LFTs, glucose, B12, TSH) were within reference limits. Prior brain MRI (2023) reportedly normal; report not yet available for direct review. No prior EEGs on file.

Workplace context: He often operates in narrow aisles with moving equipment and variable lifting loads. His supervisor has been supportive and arranged buddy cover when he feels prodromal symptoms, allowing him to sit down safely. He reports that ambient noise and bright overhead lights seem more noticeable on late shifts but cannot link them definitively to events. He carries a laminated card explaining whom to call and how to supervise him if he becomes unresponsive.

Plan: I have requested prior MRI images and will arrange a routine EEG with hyperventilation and photic stimulation, followed by an ambulatory EEG if the routine study is non-diagnostic. We discussed a potential lamotrigine titration if events continue at the current rate (q2 - 3wk), but we agreed to await EEG data first. He will continue to maintain a diary noting shift type, sleep duration, and any prodromal features. I have provided a brief workplace safety note (patient-supplied) describing general precautions already in use, which he will share with occupational health; no prescriptive restrictions were issued today. We reviewed seizure first aid and escalation steps. He knows to seek urgent care if any episode lasts more than 5 minutes or if injuries occur.

I will review him after investigations or sooner if the frequency increases or recovery time lengthens.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizure frequency is reported as q2 - 3wk over the past six months

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'Seizure frequency is reported as q2 - 3wk over the past six months.' The 'q2 - 3wk' indicates a range of every 2 to 3 weeks. No other conflicting frequency is provided. According to the formatting rules, convert this to '1 per 2 to 3 week'. The patient is not seizure-free.
seizure_frequency_number: Seizure frequency is reported as q2 - 3wk over the past six months
